title: Plastic-free bathroom swaps
subtitle: Specific substitutions with cost and longevity comparisons
excerpt: The bathroom generates a predictable stream of plastic packaging: shampoo bottles, conditioner, shower gel, cotton buds, and razors. Most have established plastic-free alternatives. This guide gives specific product types and compares cost per use against the conventional equivalent.
sourceNotes: WRAP bathroom plastics data. Ethical Consumer product comparisons. Zero Waste Week UK campaign materials.

content.text: UK homes throw out about 520 million shampoo bottles a year. Most bathroom plastic is throwaway packaging for things that can be made without it. The swaps below are sold in normal supermarkets, not just zero-waste shops. You buy them once and the habit takes care of itself.

A concentrated shampoo bar (typically 50-80 g) is equivalent to 2-3 bottles of liquid shampoo. Cost: per bar vs per bottle. Cost per wash is lower for bars because they are more concentrated. Storage: keep dry between uses, a soap dish or mesh bag prevents dissolving. Hair type matters: bars formulated for dry, fine, or coloured hair are now widely available.
Solid soap and body wash bars
Bar soap is the oldest plastic-free option. A 100 g bar lasts approximately 3-4 weeks with daily use, equivalent to around 250 ml of liquid shower gel. Cost per month: bar soap 50-80p vs shower gel .
Toothpaste tablets (in a glass jar): one tablet per brush, contains fluoride equivalent to paste. Cost comparable to standard paste per use.
Toothpaste in aluminium tubes: sold by Kingfisher and others. The paste works the same way. The metal tube is recyclable at most kerbsides.
Paper-stem cotton buds: work the same as plastic-stem ones. Sold in most shops since the UK plastic-bud ban.
Reusable cotton rounds: washable and can replace disposable cotton wool pads. One round lasts 200+ washes.

Safety razor (double-edge): a single metal handle accepts replaceable double-edge blades (10-20p each vs per cartridge). A quality handle costs and lasts indefinitely. Long-term cost 90% lower than cartridge razors.
Learning curve: two to three weeks to adapt technique. Skin irritation is common initially if pressure is applied (correct technique is zero pressure, let the weight of the handle do the work).
